name: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
tagline: The 6th-largest U.S. city — ~1.55M residents, a ~$6.8B FY2026 General Fund budget. Enterprise IT direction sits with the Office of Innovation & Technology (OIT) under the Parker administration; buying runs through the City's Procurement Department and cooperative contracts.
overview: Philadelphia is the sixth-largest city in the United States — roughly 1.55 million residents — governed by a strong-mayor system under Mayor Cherelle L. Parker and a 17-member City Council. Enterprise technology direction lives with the Office of Innovation & Technology (OIT), the City's central IT agency: it sets the citywide technology strategy, runs shared infrastructure and networks, delivers cybersecurity, and manages major technology projects for City departments. OIT is led by a Chief Information Officer who reports into the Parker administration, with a Chief Information Security Officer, Chief Data Officer, and several deputy CIOs underneath. The City is best understood as a centralized-but-departmental buyer: OIT owns enterprise infrastructure, security, and platforms, while large operational departments (Police, Fire, Revenue, Licenses & Inspections, the Water Department) carry their own systems and modernization needs. Procurement is gated centrally — competitively bid supplies and services run through the City's Procurement Department and the PHLContracts portal, with cooperative ('piggyback') contracts available to speed sourcing. For a Dell SLG seller, this is one of the largest single municipal opportunities in the Mid-Atlantic; the entry point is OIT's enterprise programs plus the Procurement Department's vehicles, not a single discretionary IT wallet.

budget.intro: Philadelphia's FY2026 'One Philly 2.0' budget was approved by City Council on June 12, 2025, with adopted General Fund expenditures of roughly $6.8B (about $6.84B), built around five pillars: public safety, clean & green, economic opportunity, housing, and education. OIT's own FY2026 General Fund budget was proposed at roughly $160M, with the year-over-year increase driven primarily by expanding cybersecurity capabilities. IT spend beyond OIT is distributed across departments rather than concentrated in one line.

stats.label: OIT FY2026 budget (proposed)
stats.note: ~$160.1M General Fund per FY26 budget testimony

stats.label: Top OIT cost driver
stats.note: FY26 increase attributed largely to expanding cybersecurity

notes: OIT's Class 200 (contracts/services) funds enterprise-wide IT — infrastructure, network, cybersecurity, productivity platforms, mobile and telecom, software engineering and licensing, and data/geodata platforms — the lines most relevant to infrastructure and software sellers.
IT spend is partly federated: large departments (Police, Fire, Revenue, L&I, Water) run their own systems and modernization, so verify which department owns a requirement before forecasting.

Grant funding matters here: a $11.9M federal NTIA Digital Equity award (Jan 2025) and other external funds support digital-equity and modernization work outside the General Fund — confirm whether a program is grant- or appropriation-backed.

initiatives.name: Citywide AI guidelines & responsible use
initiatives.summary: The City is developing AI-use guidelines for employees, with OIT working alongside the Law Department, HR, and the Chief Administrative Officer. City Council held its first hearing on government AI use in October 2025, with guidelines promised in 2026 — early signal for AI-governance, data-platform, and AI-infrastructure conversations.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity expansion
initiatives.summary: Expanding cybersecurity capability is the stated driver of OIT's FY2026 budget increase. The CISO owns citywide security strategy across networks, programs, and infrastructure — a funded, executive-priority entry point for security and resilience plays.

initiatives.name: Digital platform modernization & secure SSO
initiatives.summary: OIT modernized phila.gov and is re-platforming to expand login.phila.gov for secure resident Single Sign-On (SSO), advance mobile development, and meet new digital-accessibility standards taking effect in April 2026 — a modernization and infrastructure-refresh track.

initiatives.name: Digital equity (DigitalEquityPHL / PHLConnectED)
initiatives.summary: Backed by an $11.9M federal NTIA Digital Equity award (Jan 2025), the City is funding free/low-cost internet and devices, digital-skills training, Digital Navigators, and a network of eSports & Digital Access Centers in parks and rec facilities — device, connectivity, and endpoint demand at community scale.

procurement.intro: Selling to Philadelphia means navigating central procurement, not a single buying desk. Competitively bid supplies, equipment, and non-professional services run through the City's Procurement Department and are awarded to the lowest responsive, responsible bidder under the Home Rule Charter; professional services route through eContract Philly. The City also 'piggybacks' onto cooperative contracts established by other entities. Confirm which department owns the requirement, then map the PHLContracts path and any usable co-op vehicle.

vehicles.name: Procurement Department / PHLContracts
vehicles.detail: The City's central e-procurement system for competitively bid supplies, equipment, and non-professional services; vendors register and bid via PHLContracts, with awards governed by lowest-responsible-bidder rules.

vehicles.name: Cooperative ('piggyback') contracts
vehicles.detail: The City can buy off contracts established by other entities (e.g., the Commonwealth or national co-ops) — often the fastest path where a usable, properly scoped cooperative contract already exists.

vehicles.name: eContract Philly (professional services)
vehicles.detail: The portal for Professional Services contracts; relevant where an engagement is structured as services rather than supplies/equipment.

vehicles.name: Contracts Hub
vehicles.detail: A search tool that aggregates opportunities across the City's procurement sites — useful for tracking active and upcoming OIT and department solicitations.

powerStructure.name: Melissa A. Scott
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer (CIO), OIT
powerStructure.note: Appointed CIO by Mayor Parker in April 2024; a longtime City technologist (Revenue, L&I, OIT) with a software-development background. The top enterprise-IT relationship. A 2024 OIT restructuring eliminated several senior roles — verify current org chart before mapping.

powerStructure.name: Damian Oravez
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), OIT
powerStructure.note: Leads citywide information-security strategy and governance — the security buying-center owner, aligned to the FY2026 cybersecurity-expansion priority.

powerStructure.name: Tim Haynes
powerStructure.title: Chief Data Officer / Geographic Information Officer, OIT
powerStructure.note: Owns citywide data and geospatial platforms — the relationship for data-platform, analytics, and AI-readiness conversations.

powerStructure.name: Richard Hertz
powerStructure.title: Deputy CIO & Chief Technology Officer, OIT
powerStructure.note: Oversees technology/infrastructure direction — a key buying-center contact for infrastructure refresh and platform modernization. Confirm current title before engagement.

powerStructure.name: Cherelle L. Parker
powerStructure.title: Mayor of Philadelphia
powerStructure.note: Strong-mayor executive; OIT reports up through her administration and 'One Philly 2.0' frames the budget priorities. The executive context for any enterprise-scale program.

news.title: City Council approves ~$6.8B FY2026 'One Philly 2.0' budget
news.impact: Sets the spending envelope; OIT's increase is tied largely to cybersecurity — lead security and modernization where funded.

news.title: City Council holds first hearing on government AI use; guidelines promised
news.impact: AI governance is now an active, public priority — an opening for AI-infrastructure, data-platform, and responsible-AI conversations.

news.title: City awarded $11.9M federal NTIA Digital Equity grant
news.impact: Funds devices, connectivity, digital-skills training, and eSports/Digital Access Centers — community-scale endpoint and connectivity demand outside the General Fund.

news.title: OIT FY2026 budget testimony cites cybersecurity as primary cost driver
news.impact: Confirms security as the executive-sponsored growth area in OIT's budget — prioritize the CISO and security buying center.

news.title: Mayor Parker appoints Melissa Scott as CIO; OIT restructuring follows
news.impact: New enterprise-IT leadership with a subsequent senior-staff shakeup — rebuild and re-verify the OIT relationship map.

dellActions: Build the OIT relationship at the top — CIO Melissa Scott for enterprise strategy, plus CTO/deputy CIOs for infrastructure — and re-verify the org chart given the 2024 restructuring before mapping buying centers.
Lead with cybersecurity: it is the stated driver of OIT's FY2026 budget increase. Engage CISO Damian Oravez on security, resilience, and infrastructure-hardening.
Tie ISG / AI-infrastructure and data platforms to the City's emerging AI guidelines and the Chief Data Officer's data/geospatial programs as governance work matures in 2026.
Position endpoints and connectivity to the DigitalEquityPHL / PHLConnectED work funded by the $11.9M NTIA grant — devices, access centers, and community-scale rollouts.

Get the procurement path right early: confirm PHLContracts registration, identify usable cooperative ('piggyback') contracts, and clarify whether a requirement is supplies/equipment (Procurement Dept) or professional services (eContract Philly).
